tpha0822

Published

September 17, 2025

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This study analyses data from the survey given to the assignment with the aim to investigate relationships between various demographic, behavioral, and academic variables. The analysis will use multiple statistical techniques including hypothesis testing, correlation analysis, and regression modelling to identify significant patterns and associations within the student population. Key findings reveal significant relationships between study habits, academic performance, and lifestyle factors among university students. This research will be under the help of Perplexity, weschool (Perplexity AI 2025), (W3Schools 2025).

Motivation and Research Questions

Research Theme: Academic Performance and Student Lifestyle Factors

  • The motivation for this analysis stems from the need to understand factors that influence student academic performance and lifestyle choices in a university setting. With increasing focus on student well-being and academic success, identifying key predictors and relationships becomes crucial for educational planning and support services.

  • In this report, there will be three consecutive part determining different aspects under the form of three questions to answer our concern. These questions are:

    • Is there an association between students’ assignment approach and their target final grade?
    • Do students who pay rent study significantly more hours per week than those who don’t pay rent?
    • Do students have a systematic behavior? What toilet stall they would choose?

Data Cleaning

Data cleaning process follows closely with the given Data Cleaning guide from The University of Sydney (DATA2x02 students 2025). After data is import from excel file, all columns name are cleaned for uses. A new working data-frame called “data” was created to prepare for future needs, not all columns inside of it were cleaned if they are not used in this research. The report has three question corresponding to its essentials columns which are all cleaned, including “target_grade”, “assignment_preference”, “weekly_study_hours”, “stall_choice” for our questions. Besides, “age” and “gender” were also cleaned for EDA, but only age was used.

Library

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(gendercoder)
library(janitor)
library(hms)
library(here) 
library(dplyr)
library(gt)
library(knitr)      
library(visdat)      
library(readxl)       
library(scales)
library(plotly)
theme_set(theme_bw())

raw = readxl::read_excel(here("D:/The_University_of_sydney/DATA2002/Assignment/survey.xlsx"))

target_grade What final grade are you aiming to achieve in DATA2x02?

age How old are you?
tendency_yes_or_no Do you tend to lean towards saying "yes" or towards saying "no" to things throughout life?

stall_choice You enter a public bathroom and there are three stalls to choose from. All three are unoccupied. Which do you choose?
weetbix_count How many Weet-Bix would you typically eat in one sitting?
weekly_food_spend What is the average amount of money you spend each week on food/beverages?

weekly_alcohol How much alcohol do you consume each week?

height How tall are you?
commute How do you get to university?
daily_anxiety_frequency How often would you say you feel anxious on a daily basis?

gender What is your gender?
average_daily_sleep How much sleep do you get (on avg, per day)?
usual_bedtime What time do you usually go to bed?
sleep_schedule How consistent would you rate your sleep schedule?
sibling_count How many siblings do you have?
allergy_count How many allergies do you know you have?
diet_style What is your diet style?
random_number Pick a number at random between 1 and 10 (inclusive)
favourite_number What is your favourite number (between 1 and 10 inclusive)
favourite_letter What is your favourite letter from the English alphabet?
drivers_license Do you have a driver's license?
relationship_status Do you currently have a partner?
daily_short_video_time How much time do you spend on short video apps (like tiktok or reels) every day?

steak_preference How do you like your steak cooked?
dominant_hand What is your dominant hand?

weekly_exercise_hours On average, how many hours each week do you spend exercising?
weekly_paid_work_hours How many hours a week (on average) do you work in paid employment?
assignments_on_time Do you submit assignments on time?
used_r_before Have you ever used R before starting DATA2x02?
team_role_type What kind of role (active or passive) do you think you are when working as part of a team?
university_year Which year of university are you currently in?
favourite_anime What is your favourite anime?
fluent_languages How many languages can you speak fluently?
readable_languages How many languages can you read?
country_of_birth What country were you born in?
wam What is your WAM (weighted average mark)?
shoe_size What is your shoe size?
books_read_highschool How many books did you read in high school? (ages 12-18)
daily_water_intake_l How many litres of water do you drink on average per day?
perceived_old_age What age do you think is "old"?
study_music_preference What kind of music do you listen to when you study?

Create a clean data set for future work

  • Age column was cleaned by setting any bad values such as N/A or “unserious” value was turned into average age.

  • Gender was to Male (set as M), Female (set as F), and Others.

  • For data varaible which was used for question 1 and 2, all null value will be deleted which include “target_grade, assignment_preference, weekly_study_hours”.

  • For question 3, stall_choice was turned into a “factor” data type, then I create a table assigned to a variable called “stalled” with 3 levels “first”, “middle”, “last” representing 3 choices. Then I got rid of the column N/A since the created table included N/A because it was not cleaned in the “raw” data variable.

Code 
# Create cleaned dataset
data <- raw[, c("target_grade", "assignment_preference", "age", "gender", "university_year", "enrolled_unit", "weekly_study_hours", "work_status", "weekly_paid_work_hours", "average_daily_sleep", "usual_bedtime", "weekly_exercise_hours", "weekly_food_spend", "wam")] 
data = data %>% 
  #For any row that is totally empty, drop it. 
  remove_empty(c("rows", "cols")) 

#For every "troll" data, just convert into NA value
data$age <- ifelse(data$age > 50 | is.na(data$age) | data$age < 10, as.integer(mean(data$age, na.rm=TRUE)), data$age)
data$age <- as.integer(data$age)

data$target_grade <- ifelse(data$target_grade == "Fail", NA, data$target_grade)

#Gender cleaning
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender %in% c("NVIDIA RTX5090", "armored helicopter", "gender is a scam created by bathroom companies to sell more bathrooms. [female]", "helicopter", "assasin"), NA, data$gender) 
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender == "As in social role ? Male", "male", data$gender) 
data$gender <- toupper(data$gender)
data$gender[data$gender %in% c("FEMALE", "F", "GIRL", "FEMAL", "FAMALE")] <- "F" 
data$gender[data$gender %in% c("MALE", "M", "MALE ♂", "MAN", "BLOKE")] <- "M" 
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender %in% c("N/A", "HUMAN"), NA, data$gender) 
data$gender[!data$gender %in% c("F", "M", NA)] <- "Others"

data = data %>% drop_na(target_grade, assignment_preference, weekly_study_hours)
raw$stall_choice = as.factor(raw$stall_choice)
stall = raw$stall_choice
choice = c("first", "middle", "last") 
levels(stall) = choice
stall = stall[!is.na(stall)]

What about missing data?

The data-set itself has myriads of trouble let alone null values, for example, in column “age”, some answer in the survey is “unreal” because not all answer is seriously “answered”. These answers from age will later be converted into the “mean” of age column, including NA value. All cleaned data and value will be saved into a new data-=frame called data. Let’s have a look at the distinct age value we currently have!

Having said that, the aim of this report will be placed on performing hypothesis tests to uncover the truth from the samples survey, it does not represent the real-world situation, but at the same time it is still worth looking at. Let’s begin our testing to answer the big question.

Conducting research

Setting seed for our research

In this report, we will use seed 10 for reproducible result later!

Code 
set.seed(10)

Research 1: Are students who “cram at the last second” less likely to aim for High Distinction?

Hypothesis

\[ \begin{align*} &H_0 \text{: there is no association between study approach and targeted grade} \\ &H_1: \text{ Students who cram at the end are less likely to aim for High Distinction than students who opt for other approach} \\ \end{align*} \]

Assumptions

  • The result of the survey is randomly sampled. This is true since the survey comes from student in DATA2x02 cohort.

  • Our expected observations are not too small (expected value greater than 5).

  • a = 0.05

Checking assumptions

  • The result of the survey is randomly sample because it was sampled from student in DATA2x02 cohort.

  • Let’s check if expected frequency is over 5 for all. (In the dataset, there were some answers grade target “Fail”, I assume these are not serious answer, so all “Fail” answers are ignored in this report, we would only regard target from Pass to High Distinction, of course null values are all dropped in both of our parameters!

Code 
tab <- table(data$target_grade, data$assignment_preference) 
colnames(tab) = c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled") 
rownames(tab) = c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
tab = addmargins(tab)
Code 
observed <- matrix(c(9, 7, 15, 
                     26, 21, 52, 
                     35, 55, 100, 
                     5, 1, 6), 
                   nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)
# Calculate row totals
row_totals <- rowSums(observed)
# Calculate column totals
col_totals <- colSums(observed)

# Calculate grand total
grand_total <- sum(observed)

# Calculate expected values using formula: (row total × column total) / grand total
expected <- outer(row_totals, col_totals) / grand_total
rownames(expected) <- rownames(observed)
colnames(expected) <- colnames(observed)

print("Expected Frequencies:") 
[1] "Expected Frequencies:"
Code 
rownames(observed) <- c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
colnames(observed) <- c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled") 
rownames(expected) <- c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
colnames(expected) <- c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled")

For cases like people who would like to Cram at the last minutes and try to aim for Pass, the expected frequency is less than 5, violating what the assumption of “Chi-square test for independence”. Which means we must find alternatives test that can fit with smaller values such as Fisher’s exact test. But Fisher’s exact test only applies for 2x2 contingency tables. Then we can aim for Monte Carlo test with random seed already being set.

Code 
monte_carlo_chi_square <- function(observed, n_simulations = 10000) {
  # Calculate observed chi-square
  row_totals <- rowSums(observed)
  col_totals <- colSums(observed)
  grand_total <- sum(observed)
  expected <- outer(row_totals, col_totals) / grand_total
  
  chi_squared_obs <- sum((observed - expected)^2 / expected)
  
  # Vector to store simulated chi-square statistics
  chi_squared_sim <- numeric(n_simulations)
  
  # Run simulation
  for (i in 1:n_simulations) {
    # Generate random table with same marginal totals
    sim_table <- r2dtable(1, row_totals, col_totals)[[1]]
    sim_table <- matrix(sim_table, nrow = length(row_totals))
    
    # Calculate chi-square for simulated table
    chi_squared_sim[i] <- sum((sim_table - expected)^2 / expected)

  }
  
  # Calculate p-value
  p_value <- mean(chi_squared_sim >= chi_squared_obs)

  return(list(
    observed_chi_sq = chi_squared_obs,
    simulated_chi_sq = chi_squared_sim,
    p_value = p_value,
    n_simulations = n_simulations,
    expected = expected
  ))
}

# Run Monte Carlo simulation with YOUR data
mc_results <- monte_carlo_chi_square(observed, n_simulations = 10000)

Let’s have a look at our observed test statistics and see where it’s located when we simulate the test!

Test statistics

  • p-value: 0.2549

  • Observed test statistics: 7.770262

  • degree of freedom: (r-1) x (c-1) = (3-1) x (4-1) = 2 x 3 = 6

  • Number of simulation: 10000

Decisions

  • p > 0.05

  • Retain the hypothesis, after 10000 simulations, we cannot reject the null hypothesis stating that there is no association between target grade and assignment approach.

Test with Confidence Interval (at 95%) and Critical Value

What would be the chi-square values if we do the test again, can we predict its value, or at least know an expected value that may come off?

Code 
# Calculate 95% CI for Monte Carlo p-value
alpha = 1 - 0.95
chi_sq_ci <- quantile(mc_results$simulated_chi_sq, 
                       probs = c(alpha/2, 1-alpha/2))
critical_value <- quantile(mc_results$simulated_chi_sq, 0.95)

  • Confidence Interval at 95% confidence level: CI = [1.251572, 14.314761].

  • Critical value = 12.38341.

  • Most of the time, under H0, chi-square would be between 1.251572 and 14.314761.

  • The 95% confidence level interval for chi-square statistics under random selection is [1.251572, 14.314761], with critical value of 12.38341 at a = 0.05. Our observed chi-square statistic of 7.770262 falls within the range, so it makes sense if the above test with p-value cannot reject the null hypothesis.

Key findings

As we can see, the observed test statistics lie around the value of 8 which appear to be not too extreme and fit what the discovered statistics we already fail. A chi-square test of independence was conducted to examine the relationship between target grade and assignment approach. The test yielded χ² = 7.77 with df = 6, p = 0.2549. Since p > 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that students’ target grades are associated with their assignment approaches in this sample.”

Research 2: Do students with higher academic aspirations (High Distinction or lower grades) spend significantly more time studying per week on average.

In this question, let’s see if students who aim for High Distinction spend significantly more hours compare to the others (first we would try to use two-samples t-test to compare the mean).

Let’s create a data-frame to capture the information:

Hypothesis

\[ \begin{align*} &H_0: \text{ There is no difference between the means of weekly study hours of students who aim for High Distinction compare to the rest.} \\ &H_1: \text{ Student aiming for High Distinction study more on average.} \end{align*} \]

Significance level

This test will set significance at 0.05 for a upper tail two-samples t-test.

\[ \alpha = 0.05 \]

Assumptions

  • Our sample population follows a normal distribution

  • Study hours in both groups follows normal distribution

  • The variance of study hours is equal between both groups

  • Both group represents random samples from their respective populations

Checking assumptions

  • Let’s check if normal distribution is met with significance level (a=0.05) using Shapiro-Wilk test. First let’s have a look at our QQ-plot and have some brief ideas about our data.

  • QQ-plots:

    • Credit: Points follow the reference line reasonably well with minor deviations at tails → Possibly normal

    • Distinction: Points closely follow the line throughout → Likelynormal

    • High Distinction: A clear S-shaped curve with significant deviations → Definitely not normal, no need to perform normality test!

    • Pass: Few data points, but clear deviations from line → Likely not normal

      Code 
      qq

  • Boxplot Variance Analysis:

    • Unequal variances are clearly evident (different box sizes and whisker lengths)

    • High Distinction has much larger spread than others

    • Multiple outliers present, HD with significantly extreme outliers.

      Code 
      box

  • Density Plot Analysis:

    • Credit: Right-skewed distribution, not bell-shaped

    • Distinction: Most normal-looking, slight right skew

    • High Distinction: Heavily right-skewed with long tail

    • Pass: Too few observations, irregular shape

      Code 
      dense

Visualisations alone cannot help or confirm whether data is normally distributed or not, we still have to inspect by tests for normal distribution using Shapiro-Wilk test.

Code 
# Function to test normality for each group
test_normality <- function(data_group, group_name) { 
  sink("NUL")
  cat("\n=== Normality Tests for", group_name, "===\n")
  
  # Shapiro-Wilk test (best for small samples, n < 50)
  if(length(data_group) <= 5000) {
    shapiro_result <- shapiro.test(data_group)
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk Test:\n")
    cat("W =", round(shapiro_result$statistic, 4), 
        ", p-value =", format(shapiro_result$p.value, scientific = TRUE), "\n")
  } else {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk Test: Skipped (n > 5000)\n")
  }
  
  
  # Interpretation
  cat("\nInterpretation (α = 0.05):\n")
  if(exists("shapiro_result") && shapiro_result$p.value > 0.05) {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk: Normal distribution (p > 0.05)\n")
  } else if(exists("shapiro_result")) {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk: Non-normal distribution (p ≤ 0.05)\n")
  }
  sink() 
}

# Test each group
test_normality(hd, "HD")
test_normality(p, "Pass") 
test_normality(c, "Credit")
test_normality(d, "Distinction")

None of the groups are normally distributed which mean we should opt for other non-parametric tests such as Wilcoxon Rank-sum or Signed-rank tests. Here we will use pairwise Mann-Whitney U Tests (also known as Rank-sum test)

  • Since we know our data is created in the survey, the data is independent of each other!

Performing Tests

Code 
# Manual Mann-Whitney U Test Function
manual_mann_whitney <- function(group1, group2) {
  # Step 1: Combine data with group labels
  combined_data <- c(group1, group2)
  group_labels <- c(rep("Group1", length(group1)), 
                   rep("Group2", length(group2)))
  
  # Step 2: Rank all combined data
  ranks <- rank(combined_data, ties.method = "average")
  
  # Step 3: Separate ranks by group
  ranks_group1 <- ranks[group_labels == "Group1"]
  ranks_group2 <- ranks[group_labels == "Group2"]
  
  # Step 4: Calculate rank sums
  R1 <- sum(ranks_group1)
  R2 <- sum(ranks_group2)
  
  n1 <- length(group1)
  n2 <- length(group2)
  N <- n1 + n2  # Total sample size
  
  # Step 5: Calculate U statistics
  expected_W <- n1 * (N + 1) / 2
  U1 <- n1 * n2 + n1 * (n1 + 1) / 2 - R1
  U2 <- n1 * n2 + n2 * (n2 + 1) / 2 - R2
  
  # Step 6: Test statistic (minimum U)
  U <- U1
  
  # Step 7: Normal approximation (for large samples)
  mu_U <- (n1 * n2) / 2
  sigma_U <- sqrt((n1 * n2 * (n1 + n2 + 1)) / 12)
  z_score <- (U - mu_U) / sigma_U
  
  # Step 8: P-value (two-tailed)
  p_value <- pnorm(abs(z_score), lower.tail = FALSE)
  
  # Return results
  list(
    U1 = U1,
    U2 = U2,
    U = U,
    z_score = z_score,
    p_value = p_value,
    n1 = n1,
    n2 = n2,
    W1 = R1,
    W2 = R2,
    ranks_group1 = ranks_group1,
    ranks_group2 = ranks_group2, 
    eW = expected_W
  )
}
hd_p = manual_mann_whitney(hd, p)
hd_c = manual_mann_whitney(hd, c)
hd_d = manual_mann_whitney(hd, d)
# Example with your HD vs other groups
# Assuming you have vectors hd_data and other_data
# result <- manual_mann_whitney(hd_data, other_data)
# print(result)

Test statistics

High Distinction vs Distinction
\(W_{hdd} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 29750.5\)
High Distinction vs Credit

\(W_{hdc} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 23084\)

High Distinction vs Pass
\(W_{hdp} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 21094\)

Observed Test Statistic

High Distinction vs Distinction

  • \(w_{hdd} = 32502\)

  • P-value: \(\text{P}(W \geq w) << 0.05\)

High Distinction vs Credit

  • \(w_{hdc} = 24123\)

  • P-value: \(\text{P}(W \geq w) < 0.05\)

High Distinction vs Pass

  • \(w_{hdp} = 21094\)

  • P-value: \(\text{P}(W \geq w)< 0.05\)

Decision

  • Since p-value < 0.05 in all cases, there is a strong evidence that HD students tend to study more hours than the comparison group since we reject H0.

  • Now let’s try and run the test with built-in function.

    Code 
    # Test HD vs each group separately (one-tailed tests)
test_hd_vs_distinction <- wilcox.test(hd, d, alternative = "greater")
test_hd_vs_credit <- wilcox.test(hd, c, alternative = "greater") 
test_hd_vs_pass <- wilcox.test(hd, p, alternative = "greater")

# View results
cat("HD vs D:", test_hd_vs_distinction$p.value)
    HD vs D: 4.104403e-05
    Code 
    cat("HD vs C", test_hd_vs_credit$p.value)
    HD vs C 0.001491652
    Code 
    cat("HD vs P", test_hd_vs_pass$p.value)
    HD vs P 0.004230571

The p-value seems to fit what we already done above!

Key findings

The analysis revealed statistically significant differences (p < 0.05) in study behavior patterns, with HD aspirers consistently demonstrating higher weekly study hours compared to all other grade categories examined.

These findings support the hypothesis that academic success at the highest level (HD) requires correspondingly higher time investment in study activities. This has important implications for:

  • Academic counseling: Students should be informed about the study time commitment typically associated with HD-level performance

  • Expectation management: Clear communication about the relationship between effort and achievement outcomes

Research 3: Bathroom Stall Selection Patterns

The report has been quite dense with statistics, so let’s switch gears and add a little fun into the mix! Let’s explore a more lighthearted question: How do students in DATA2x02 choose their bathroom stalls? Are their choices systematic and consistent, or are they completely random? By examining this everyday decision-making process, we might uncover some unexpected patterns.

There’s some interesting research that suggests people tend to avoid the first stall due to a perceived lack of privacy, while others gravitate toward the middle option for a sense of security. Could these tendencies hold true for our students? By investigating their choices, we might uncover valuable insights into how people make decisions in seemingly mundane situations.

To test this, we’ll perform a Chi-square goodness-of-fit test to determine whether students’ stall selections align with random chance or follow a more structured pattern.

Hypothesis

\(H_0: \text{ The stall preference are random, there is no bias toward any stall}\)

\(H_1: \text{ Data2x02 show systematic preference towards a certain stall}\)

Significance Level

\[ \alpha=0.05 \]

Assumptions

  • The populations are independent

  • Expected count for each choice is not any smaller than 5 (\(e_i = n p_i \geq 5\))

Visualisations

Code 
df = tibble( 
  stall_choice = c("first", "middle", "last"), 
  y = c(85, 81, 199), 
  p = c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3), 
  e = sum(y) * p)
Code 
df_comparison <- data.frame(
  stall = rep(c("First", "Middle", "Last"), 2),
  count = c(85, 81, 199, 121.67, 121.67, 121.67),
  type = rep(c("Observed", "Expected"), each = 3)
)

ggplot(df_comparison, aes(x = stall, y = count, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(count)), position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Bathroom Stall Choice: Observed vs. Expected Under Random Selection",
       subtitle = "Strong preference for privacy-providing last stall",
       x = "Stall Position", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

First and Middle stall are less likely to be chosen but the Last one is spefically high when being visualised.

Checking assumptions

  • Under null hypothesis, \(p_{first} =p_{middle} = p_{last} = p = 1/3 \text{ => } e_{first} = e_{middle} = e_{last} = e = n.p\) .

  • Since e = 121.6667, we are sure that none assumptions are violated.

Observed Test Statistics

Code 
y = df$y 
e = df$e
#Observed Test Statistic
t0 = sum((y - e)^2/e)
#p-value 
pval = pchisq(t0, df = 2, lower.tail = F)

  • Test statistic: \(t_0 = \sum^3_{i=1} \frac{(y_i-e_i)^2}{e_i} = 73.79726\)

  • Degree of freedom: df = k - 1 - q = 3 - 1 - 0 = 2

  • P-value: P(\(\chi^2_2 \geq 73.79726\)) << 0.05

Decision

  • Since p << 0.05, there is a strong evidence to reject the null hypothesis, people don’t randomly choose the stall but rather having a specific preference towards the third stall.

Some external studies about behaviors

In addition to our own investigation, external studies offer valuable perspectives that can deepen our understanding of student behavior. One such study by Heston (2023) delves into the psychology behind bathroom stall selection, revealing patterns that may have broader implications for how students make decisions in academic and lifestyle contexts.

Heston’s research found that people tend to choose the last stall, often due to the increased sense of privacy it provides. As Heston notes (Heston 2023):
“The findings in this study support the hypothesis that people seek security when choosing which bathroom stall to use. The top choice in this study, which occurred twice as often as the other two options, was the stall with a solid wall on one side and only one exposed partition. This preference makes psychological sense, as people are drawn to the stall that offers greater privacy than the others.”

This preference for privacy and security can be linked to broader student behaviors, particularly in academic and lifestyle choices. Just as students might opt for the bathroom stall that feels most secure, they may also seek out environments that provide comfort and control over their surroundings. In academic settings, this could manifest in students preferring smaller, quieter study spaces or choosing professors who create a more personalized, supportive classroom atmosphere. In lifestyle choices, it may reflect a tendency to gravitate toward habits or routines that offer a sense of stability and reduced uncertainty, whether it’s in their social interactions, study habits, or even daily schedules.

The strong evidence for systematic spatial choices has practical implications for university facility design. Students’ clear preference for privacy-providing options suggests that learning spaces, study areas, and social environments should incorporate varied privacy levels to optimize student comfort and well-being.

References

DATA2x02 students. 2025. “DATA2x02 Class Survey.” 2025. https://canvas.sydney.edu.au/courses/67975/files/45130664?wrap=1.
Heston, Thomas F. 2023. “Selecting the Safest Public Bathroom Stall in the Era of COVID-19.” Journal of Clinical Medical Research 4 (2): 1–4. https://doi.org/10.46889/JCMR/2023.4215.
Perplexity AI, Inc. 2025. “Perplexity AI.” https://www.perplexity.ai/.
W3Schools. 2025. “R Tutorial.” https://www.w3schools.com/r/.
Source Code

title: "Assignment 1"
date: "`r format(Sys.time(), '%d %B, %Y')`"
author: "tpha0822"
format: 
  html: 
    embed-resources: true # Creates a single HTML file as output
    code-fold: true # Code folding; allows you to show/hide code chunks
    code-tools: true # Includes a menu to download the code file 
    # code-tools are particularly important if you use inline R to 
    # improve the reproducibility of your report
table-of-contents: true # (Optional) Creates a table of contents
number-sections: false # (Optional) Puts numbers next to heading/subheadings 
bibliography: references.bib 
---

## EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

This study analyses data from the survey given to the assignment with the aim to investigate relationships between various demographic, behavioral, and academic variables. The analysis will use multiple statistical techniques including hypothesis testing, correlation analysis, and regression modelling to identify significant patterns and associations within the student population. Key findings reveal significant relationships between study habits, academic performance, and lifestyle factors among university students. This research will be under the help of Perplexity, weschool [@perplexity_ai], [@w3schools_r_tutorial].

## Motivation and Research Questions

### Research Theme: Academic Performance and Student Lifestyle Factors

-   The motivation for this analysis stems from the need to understand factors that influence student academic performance and lifestyle choices in a university setting. With increasing focus on student well-being and academic success, identifying key predictors and relationships becomes crucial for educational planning and support services.

-   In this report, there will be three consecutive part determining different aspects under the form of three questions to answer our concern. These questions are:

    -   Is there an association between students' assignment approach and their target final grade?
    -   Do students who pay rent study significantly more hours per week than those who don't pay rent?
    -   Do students have a systematic behavior? What toilet stall they would choose?

## Data Cleaning

Data cleaning process follows closely with the given Data Cleaning guide from The University of Sydney [@data2002survey]. After data is import from excel file, all columns name are cleaned for uses. A new working data-frame called "data" was created to prepare for future needs, not all columns inside of it were cleaned if they are not used in this research. The report has three question corresponding to its essentials columns which are all cleaned, including "target_grade", "assignment_preference", "weekly_study_hours", "stall_choice" for our questions. Besides, "age" and "gender" were also cleaned for EDA, but only age was used.

**Library**

```{r, message=FALSE}
library(tidyverse)
library(gendercoder)
library(janitor)
library(hms)
library(here) 
library(dplyr)
library(gt)
library(knitr)      
library(visdat)      
library(readxl)       
library(scales)
library(plotly)
theme_set(theme_bw())
```

**Import**

```{r}
raw = readxl::read_excel(here("D:/The_University_of_sydney/DATA2002/Assignment/survey.xlsx"))
```

**Clean columns' name**

```{r}
old_names = colnames(raw)
new_names <- c(
  "timestamp",
  "target_grade",
  "assignment_preference",
  "trimester_or_semester",
  "age",
  "tendency_yes_or_no",
  "pay_rent",
  "stall_choice",
  "weetbix_count",
  "weekly_food_spend",
  "living_arrangements",
  "weekly_alcohol",
  "believe_in_aliens",
  "height",
  "commute",
  "daily_anxiety_frequency",
  "weekly_study_hours",
  "work_status",
  "social_media",
  "gender",
  "average_daily_sleep",
  "usual_bedtime",
  "sleep_schedule",
  "sibling_count",
  "allergy_count",
  "diet_style",
  "random_number",
  "favourite_number",
  "favourite_letter",
  "drivers_license",
  "relationship_status",
  "daily_short_video_time",
  "computer_os",
  "steak_preference",
  "dominant_hand",
  "enrolled_unit",
  "weekly_exercise_hours",
  "weekly_paid_work_hours",
  "assignments_on_time",
  "used_r_before",
  "team_role_type",
  "university_year",
  "favourite_anime",
  "fluent_languages",
  "readable_languages",
  "country_of_birth",
  "wam",
  "shoe_size",
  "books_read_highschool",
  "daily_water_intake_l",
  "perceived_old_age",
  "study_music_preference"
)
# overwrite the old names with the new names:
colnames(raw) = new_names
# combine old and new into a data frame:
name_combo = bind_cols(New = new_names, Old = old_names)
name_combo |> gt::gt()
```

**Create a clean data set for future work**

-   Age column was cleaned by setting any bad values such as N/A or "unserious" value was turned into average age.

-   Gender was to Male (set as M), Female (set as F), and Others.

-   For data varaible which was used for question 1 and 2, all null value will be deleted which include "target_grade, assignment_preference, weekly_study_hours".

-   For question 3, stall_choice was turned into a "factor" data type, then I create a table assigned to a variable called "stalled" with 3 levels "first", "middle", "last" representing 3 choices. Then I got rid of the column N/A since the created table included N/A because it was not cleaned in the "raw" data variable.

```{r}
# Create cleaned dataset
data <- raw[, c("target_grade", "assignment_preference", "age", "gender", "university_year", "enrolled_unit", "weekly_study_hours", "work_status", "weekly_paid_work_hours", "average_daily_sleep", "usual_bedtime", "weekly_exercise_hours", "weekly_food_spend", "wam")] 
data = data %>% 
  #For any row that is totally empty, drop it. 
  remove_empty(c("rows", "cols")) 

#For every "troll" data, just convert into NA value
data$age <- ifelse(data$age > 50 | is.na(data$age) | data$age < 10, as.integer(mean(data$age, na.rm=TRUE)), data$age)
data$age <- as.integer(data$age)

data$target_grade <- ifelse(data$target_grade == "Fail", NA, data$target_grade)

#Gender cleaning
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender %in% c("NVIDIA RTX5090", "armored helicopter", "gender is a scam created by bathroom companies to sell more bathrooms. [female]", "helicopter", "assasin"), NA, data$gender) 
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender == "As in social role ? Male", "male", data$gender) 
data$gender <- toupper(data$gender)
data$gender[data$gender %in% c("FEMALE", "F", "GIRL", "FEMAL", "FAMALE")] <- "F" 
data$gender[data$gender %in% c("MALE", "M", "MALE ♂", "MAN", "BLOKE")] <- "M" 
data$gender <- ifelse(data$gender %in% c("N/A", "HUMAN"), NA, data$gender) 
data$gender[!data$gender %in% c("F", "M", NA)] <- "Others"

data = data %>% drop_na(target_grade, assignment_preference, weekly_study_hours)
raw$stall_choice = as.factor(raw$stall_choice)
stall = raw$stall_choice
choice = c("first", "middle", "last") 
levels(stall) = choice
stall = stall[!is.na(stall)]
```

## Exploratory Data Analysis

-   Before we start the investigation, let's first have a look at our survey data, the columns from raw data have already been cleaned in advance to looks nicer. The below information would be the summary of our data.

```{r}
#| fig.width: 6
#| fig.height: 5
#summary(raw)
head(raw, 3) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(title = "First 5 Rows of Survey Data") %>%
  tab_options(table.font.size = 10) 
```

What about missing data?

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| fig.width: 6
#| fig.height: 10
high_miss_vars <- raw %>%
  summarise_all(~sum(is.na(.)) / length(.) * 100) %>%
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "variable", values_to = "pct_miss") %>%
  filter(pct_miss > 5) %>%
  pull(variable)

# Plot only these variables
raw %>%
  select(all_of(high_miss_vars)) %>%
  visdat::vis_miss(sort_miss = TRUE) +
  labs(title = "Variables with >5% Missing Data") + 
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90, hjust = 0))
```

The data-set itself has myriads of trouble let alone null values, for example, in column "age", some answer in the survey is "unreal" because not all answer is seriously "answered". These answers from age will later be converted into the "mean" of age column, including NA value. All cleaned data and value will be saved into a new data-=frame called data. Let's have a look at the distinct age value we currently have!

```{r, echo=FALSE}
#| fig.width: 6
#| fig.height: 5

data |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = age)) + 
  geom_histogram(aes(fill = after_stat(count)), color = "black", bins = 30) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#FF6B35",      # Orange
    mid = "#A855F7",      # Purple  
    high = "#06B6D4",     # Teal
    midpoint = median(hist(data$age, plot = FALSE)$counts)
  ) +
  labs(x = "Age", y = "Count", fill = "Frequency") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14)
  

```

Having said that, the aim of this report will be placed on performing hypothesis tests to uncover the truth from the samples survey, it does not represent the real-world situation, but at the same time it is still worth looking at. Let's begin our testing to answer the big question.

## Conducting research

### Setting seed for our research

In this report, we will use seed 10 for reproducible result later!

```{r}
set.seed(10)
```

### Research 1: Are students who "cram at the last second" less likely to aim for High Distinction?

#### Hypothesis

$$ 
\begin{align*}
  &H_0 \text{: there is no association between study approach and targeted grade} \\
  &H_1: \text{ Students who cram at the end are less likely to aim for High Distinction than students who opt for other approach} \\ 
\end{align*}
$$

#### Assumptions

-   The result of the survey is randomly sampled. This is true since the survey comes from student in DATA2x02 cohort.

-   Our expected observations are not too small (expected value greater than 5).

-   a = 0.05\

#### Checking assumptions

-   The result of the survey is randomly sample because it was sampled from student in DATA2x02 cohort.

-   Let's check if expected frequency is over 5 for all. (In the dataset, there were some answers grade target "Fail", I assume these are not serious answer, so all "Fail" answers are ignored in this report, we would only regard target from Pass to High Distinction, of course null values are all dropped in both of our parameters!

```{r}
tab <- table(data$target_grade, data$assignment_preference) 
colnames(tab) = c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled") 
rownames(tab) = c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
tab = addmargins(tab)

```

```{r}
observed <- matrix(c(9, 7, 15, 
                     26, 21, 52, 
                     35, 55, 100, 
                     5, 1, 6), 
                   nrow = 4, byrow = TRUE)
# Calculate row totals
row_totals <- rowSums(observed)
# Calculate column totals
col_totals <- colSums(observed)

# Calculate grand total
grand_total <- sum(observed)

# Calculate expected values using formula: (row total × column total) / grand total
expected <- outer(row_totals, col_totals) / grand_total
rownames(expected) <- rownames(observed)
colnames(expected) <- colnames(observed)

print("Expected Frequencies:") 
rownames(observed) <- c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
colnames(observed) <- c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled") 
rownames(expected) <- c("C", "D", "HD", "P")
colnames(expected) <- c("Cram", "Immediately", "Scheduled") 
```

For cases like people who would like to Cram at the last minutes and try to aim for Pass, the expected frequency is less than 5, violating what the assumption of "Chi-square test for independence". Which means we must find alternatives test that can fit with smaller values such as Fisher's exact test. But Fisher's exact test only applies for 2x2 contingency tables. Then we can aim for Monte Carlo test with random seed already being set.

```{r}
monte_carlo_chi_square <- function(observed, n_simulations = 10000) {
  # Calculate observed chi-square
  row_totals <- rowSums(observed)
  col_totals <- colSums(observed)
  grand_total <- sum(observed)
  expected <- outer(row_totals, col_totals) / grand_total
  
  chi_squared_obs <- sum((observed - expected)^2 / expected)
  
  # Vector to store simulated chi-square statistics
  chi_squared_sim <- numeric(n_simulations)
  
  # Run simulation
  for (i in 1:n_simulations) {
    # Generate random table with same marginal totals
    sim_table <- r2dtable(1, row_totals, col_totals)[[1]]
    sim_table <- matrix(sim_table, nrow = length(row_totals))
    
    # Calculate chi-square for simulated table
    chi_squared_sim[i] <- sum((sim_table - expected)^2 / expected)

  }
  
  # Calculate p-value
  p_value <- mean(chi_squared_sim >= chi_squared_obs)

  return(list(
    observed_chi_sq = chi_squared_obs,
    simulated_chi_sq = chi_squared_sim,
    p_value = p_value,
    n_simulations = n_simulations,
    expected = expected
  ))
}

# Run Monte Carlo simulation with YOUR data
mc_results <- monte_carlo_chi_square(observed, n_simulations = 10000)

```

Let's have a look at our observed test statistics and see where it's located when we simulate the test!

```{r echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
#| fig-height: 7
#| fig-width: 8


mc_data <- data.frame(chi_square = mc_results$simulated_chi_sq)

# Enhanced histogram with better color scheme and styling
ggplot(mc_data, aes(x = chi_square)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, 
                 fill = "#66c2a5",           # Teal-green color
                 color = "#2c3e50",          # Dark border
                 alpha = 0.85,               # Slightly more opaque
                 linewidth = 0.3) +
  
  # Enhanced vertical line
  geom_vline(xintercept = 7.770262, 
             color = "#e74c3c",             # Vibrant red-orange
             linewidth = 2.5,
             linetype = "solid") +

  
  # Enhanced labels and title
  labs(title = "Monte Carlo Simulation: Chi-square Test Statistics",
       subtitle = paste("Based on", length(mc_results$simulated_chi_sq), "simulations"),
       x = "Simulated Chi-square Test Statistics", 
       y = "Frequency",
       caption = "Red line: Observed statistic") +
  
  # Professional theme with custom styling
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5, color = "#2c3e50"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5, color = "#34495e", margin = margin(b = 20)),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 10, color = "#7f8c8d", hjust = 0),
    
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", color = "#2c3e50"),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11, color = "#34495e"),
    
    panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", size = 0.5),
    panel.grid.minor = element_line(color = "#ecf0f1", size = 0.25),
    
    plot.background = element_rect(fill = "white", color = NA),
    panel.background = element_rect(fill = "#fafafa", color = NA),
    
    plot.margin = margin(t = 20, r = 20, b = 20, l = 20)
  ) 
```

#### Test statistics

-   p-value: 0.2549

-   Observed test statistics: 7.770262

-   degree of freedom: (r-1) x (c-1) = (3-1) x (4-1) = 2 x 3 = 6

-   Number of simulation: 10000

#### Decisions

-   p \> 0.05

-   Retain the hypothesis, after 10000 simulations, we cannot reject the null hypothesis stating that there is no association between target grade and assignment approach.

#### Test with Confidence Interval (at 95%) and Critical Value  

What would be the chi-square values if we do the test again, can we predict its value, or at least know an expected value that may come off?

```{r}
# Calculate 95% CI for Monte Carlo p-value
alpha = 1 - 0.95
chi_sq_ci <- quantile(mc_results$simulated_chi_sq, 
                       probs = c(alpha/2, 1-alpha/2))
critical_value <- quantile(mc_results$simulated_chi_sq, 0.95)
```

-   Confidence Interval at 95% confidence level: CI = \[1.251572, 14.314761\].

-   Critical value = 12.38341.

-   Most of the time, under H0, chi-square would be between 1.251572 and 14.314761.

-   The 95% confidence level interval for chi-square statistics under random selection is \[1.251572, 14.314761\], with critical value of 12.38341 at a = 0.05. Our observed chi-square statistic of 7.770262 falls within the range, so it makes sense if the above test with p-value cannot reject the null hypothesis.

#### Key findings

As we can see, the observed test statistics lie around the value of 8 which appear to be not too extreme and fit what the discovered statistics we already fail. A chi-square test of independence was conducted to examine the relationship between target grade and assignment approach. The test yielded χ² = 7.77 with df = 6, p = 0.2549. Since p \> 0.05, we fail to reject the null hypothesis. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that students' target grades are associated with their assignment approaches in this sample."

### Research 2: Do students with higher academic aspirations (High Distinction or lower grades) spend significantly more time studying per week on average.

In this question, let's see if students who aim for High Distinction spend significantly more hours compare to the others (first we would try to use two-samples t-test to compare the mean).

Let's create a data-frame to capture the information:

```{r, echo=FALSE}
hd <- data %>% 
  filter(target_grade == "High Distinction") %>% 
  pull(weekly_study_hours) 
c <- data %>% 
  filter(target_grade == "Credit") %>%
  pull(weekly_study_hours) 
p <- data %>% 
  filter(target_grade == "Pass") %>%
  pull(weekly_study_hours) 
d <- data %>% 
  filter(target_grade == "Distinction") %>%
  pull(weekly_study_hours) 

```

#### Hypothesis

$$
\begin{align*}
&H_0: \text{ There is no difference between the means of weekly study hours of students who aim for High Distinction compare to the rest.} \\ 
&H_1: \text{ Student aiming for High Distinction study more on average.} 
\end{align*}
$$

#### Significance level

This test will set significance at 0.05 for a upper tail two-samples t-test.

$$
\alpha = 0.05
$$

#### Assumptions

-   Our sample population follows a normal distribution

-   Study hours in both groups follows normal distribution

-   The variance of study hours is equal between both groups

-   Both group represents random samples from their respective populations

#### Checking assumptions

-   Let's check if normal distribution is met with significance level (a=0.05) using Shapiro-Wilk test. First let's have a look at our QQ-plot and have some brief ideas about our data.

    ```{r, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
    #| fig.width: 6
    #| fig.height: 5
    # Create multiple normality plots
    par(mfrow = c(2, 2))

    # Q-Q plots by group
    qq <- ggplot(data, aes(sample = weekly_study_hours)) +
      stat_qq() + 
      stat_qq_line(color = "red") +
      facet_wrap(~ target_grade, scales = "free") +
      labs(title = "Q-Q Plots by Grade Category") +
      theme_minimal()

    # Boxplots for overall distribution
    box <- ggplot(data, aes(x = target_grade, y = weekly_study_hours, fill = target_grade)) +
      geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
      labs(title = "Boxplots by Grade Category",
           x = "Grade Category", y = "Study Hours") +
      theme_minimal()

    # Density plots overlaid
    dense <- ggplot(data, aes(x = weekly_study_hours, fill = target_grade)) +
      geom_density(alpha = 0.5) +
      facet_wrap(~ target_grade, scales = "free") +
      labs(title = "Density Plots by Grade Category",
           x = "Study Hours", y = "Density") +
      theme_minimal()


    ```

-   **QQ-plots**:

    -   Credit: Points follow the reference line reasonably well with minor deviations at tails → Possibly normal

    -   Distinction: Points closely follow the line throughout → Likelynormal

    -   High Distinction: A clear S-shaped curve with significant deviations → Definitely not normal, no need to perform normality test!

    -   Pass: Few data points, but clear deviations from line → Likely not normal

        ```{r}
        qq
        ```

-   **Boxplot Variance Analysis:**

    -   **Unequal variances** are clearly evident (different box sizes and whisker lengths)

    -   High Distinction has much larger spread than others

    -   Multiple outliers present, HD with significantly extreme outliers.

        ```{r}
        box
        ```

-   **Density Plot Analysis:**

    -   Credit: Right-skewed distribution, not bell-shaped

    -   Distinction: Most normal-looking, slight right skew

    -   High Distinction: Heavily right-skewed with long tail

    -   Pass: Too few observations, irregular shape

        ```{r}
        dense
        ```

Visualisations alone cannot help or confirm whether data is normally distributed or not, we still have to inspect by tests for normal distribution using Shapiro-Wilk test.

```{r, message=FALSE, result='hide', warning=FALSE}
# Function to test normality for each group
test_normality <- function(data_group, group_name) { 
  sink("NUL")
  cat("\n=== Normality Tests for", group_name, "===\n")
  
  # Shapiro-Wilk test (best for small samples, n < 50)
  if(length(data_group) <= 5000) {
    shapiro_result <- shapiro.test(data_group)
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk Test:\n")
    cat("W =", round(shapiro_result$statistic, 4), 
        ", p-value =", format(shapiro_result$p.value, scientific = TRUE), "\n")
  } else {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk Test: Skipped (n > 5000)\n")
  }
  
  
  # Interpretation
  cat("\nInterpretation (α = 0.05):\n")
  if(exists("shapiro_result") && shapiro_result$p.value > 0.05) {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk: Normal distribution (p > 0.05)\n")
  } else if(exists("shapiro_result")) {
    cat("Shapiro-Wilk: Non-normal distribution (p ≤ 0.05)\n")
  }
  sink() 
}

# Test each group
test_normality(hd, "HD")
test_normality(p, "Pass") 
test_normality(c, "Credit")
test_normality(d, "Distinction")

```

None of the groups are normally distributed which mean we should opt for other non-parametric tests such as Wilcoxon Rank-sum or Signed-rank tests. Here we will use pairwise Mann-Whitney U Tests (also known as Rank-sum test)

-   Since we know our data is created in the survey, the data is independent of each other!

### Performing Tests

```{r, message=FALSE}
# Manual Mann-Whitney U Test Function
manual_mann_whitney <- function(group1, group2) {
  # Step 1: Combine data with group labels
  combined_data <- c(group1, group2)
  group_labels <- c(rep("Group1", length(group1)), 
                   rep("Group2", length(group2)))
  
  # Step 2: Rank all combined data
  ranks <- rank(combined_data, ties.method = "average")
  
  # Step 3: Separate ranks by group
  ranks_group1 <- ranks[group_labels == "Group1"]
  ranks_group2 <- ranks[group_labels == "Group2"]
  
  # Step 4: Calculate rank sums
  R1 <- sum(ranks_group1)
  R2 <- sum(ranks_group2)
  
  n1 <- length(group1)
  n2 <- length(group2)
  N <- n1 + n2  # Total sample size
  
  # Step 5: Calculate U statistics
  expected_W <- n1 * (N + 1) / 2
  U1 <- n1 * n2 + n1 * (n1 + 1) / 2 - R1
  U2 <- n1 * n2 + n2 * (n2 + 1) / 2 - R2
  
  # Step 6: Test statistic (minimum U)
  U <- U1
  
  # Step 7: Normal approximation (for large samples)
  mu_U <- (n1 * n2) / 2
  sigma_U <- sqrt((n1 * n2 * (n1 + n2 + 1)) / 12)
  z_score <- (U - mu_U) / sigma_U
  
  # Step 8: P-value (two-tailed)
  p_value <- pnorm(abs(z_score), lower.tail = FALSE)
  
  # Return results
  list(
    U1 = U1,
    U2 = U2,
    U = U,
    z_score = z_score,
    p_value = p_value,
    n1 = n1,
    n2 = n2,
    W1 = R1,
    W2 = R2,
    ranks_group1 = ranks_group1,
    ranks_group2 = ranks_group2, 
    eW = expected_W
  )
}
hd_p = manual_mann_whitney(hd, p)
hd_c = manual_mann_whitney(hd, c)
hd_d = manual_mann_whitney(hd, d)
# Example with your HD vs other groups
# Assuming you have vectors hd_data and other_data
# result <- manual_mann_whitney(hd_data, other_data)
# print(result)

```

#### Test statistics

##### High Distinction vs Distinction

##### $W_{hdd} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 29750.5$

##### High Distinction vs Credit

$W_{hdc} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 23084$

##### High Distinction vs Pass

##### $W_{hdp} = n_{hd} . \frac{N+1}{2} = 21094$

#### Observed Test Statistic

##### High Distinction vs Distinction

-   $w_{hdd} = 32502$

-   **P-value:** $\text{P}(W \geq w) << 0.05$

##### High Distinction vs Credit

-   $w_{hdc} = 24123$

-   **P-value:** $\text{P}(W \geq w) < 0.05$

##### High Distinction vs Pass

-   $w_{hdp} = 21094$

-   **P-value:** $\text{P}(W \geq w)< 0.05$

#### Decision

-   Since p-value \< 0.05 in all cases, there is a **strong evidence** that HD students tend to study more hours than the comparison group since we reject H0.

-   Now let's try and run the test with built-in function.

    ```{r}
    # Test HD vs each group separately (one-tailed tests)
    test_hd_vs_distinction <- wilcox.test(hd, d, alternative = "greater")
    test_hd_vs_credit <- wilcox.test(hd, c, alternative = "greater") 
    test_hd_vs_pass <- wilcox.test(hd, p, alternative = "greater")

    # View results
    cat("HD vs D:", test_hd_vs_distinction$p.value)
    cat("HD vs C", test_hd_vs_credit$p.value)
    cat("HD vs P", test_hd_vs_pass$p.value)

    ```

The p-value seems to fit what we already done above!

#### Key findings

The analysis revealed statistically significant differences (p \< 0.05) in study behavior patterns, with HD aspirers consistently demonstrating higher weekly study hours compared to all other grade categories examined.

These findings support the hypothesis that academic success at the highest level (HD) requires correspondingly higher time investment in study activities. This has important implications for:

-   **Academic counseling**: Students should be informed about the study time commitment typically associated with HD-level performance

-   **Expectation management**: Clear communication about the relationship between effort and achievement outcomes

### Research 3: Bathroom Stall Selection Patterns

The report has been quite dense with statistics, so let’s switch gears and add a little fun into the mix! Let’s explore a more lighthearted question: How do students in DATA2x02 choose their bathroom stalls? Are their choices systematic and consistent, or are they completely random? By examining this everyday decision-making process, we might uncover some unexpected patterns.

There’s some interesting research that suggests people tend to avoid the first stall due to a perceived lack of privacy, while others gravitate toward the middle option for a sense of security. Could these tendencies hold true for our students? By investigating their choices, we might uncover valuable insights into how people make decisions in seemingly mundane situations.

To test this, we’ll perform a Chi-square goodness-of-fit test to determine whether students’ stall selections align with random chance or follow a more structured pattern.

#### Hypothesis

$H_0: \text{ The stall preference are random, there is no bias toward any stall}$

$H_1: \text{ Data2x02 show systematic preference towards a certain stall}$

#### Significance Level

$$
\alpha=0.05
$$

#### Assumptions

-   The populations are independent

-   Expected count for each choice is not any smaller than 5 ($e_i = n p_i \geq 5$)

#### Visualisations

```{r}
df = tibble( 
  stall_choice = c("first", "middle", "last"), 
  y = c(85, 81, 199), 
  p = c(1/3, 1/3, 1/3), 
  e = sum(y) * p)  
```

```{r}
df_comparison <- data.frame(
  stall = rep(c("First", "Middle", "Last"), 2),
  count = c(85, 81, 199, 121.67, 121.67, 121.67),
  type = rep(c("Observed", "Expected"), each = 3)
)

ggplot(df_comparison, aes(x = stall, y = count, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(count)), position = position_dodge(width = 0.9), vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Bathroom Stall Choice: Observed vs. Expected Under Random Selection",
       subtitle = "Strong preference for privacy-providing last stall",
       x = "Stall Position", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
```

First and Middle stall are less likely to be chosen but the Last one is spefically high when being visualised.

#### Checking assumptions

-   Under null hypothesis, $p_{first} =p_{middle} = p_{last} = p = 1/3 \text{ => } e_{first} = e_{middle} = e_{last} = e = n.p$ .

-   Since e = 121.6667, we are sure that none assumptions are violated.

#### Observed Test Statistics

```{r}
y = df$y 
e = df$e
#Observed Test Statistic
t0 = sum((y - e)^2/e)
#p-value 
pval = pchisq(t0, df = 2, lower.tail = F) 

```

-   **Test statistic:** $t_0 = \sum^3_{i=1} \frac{(y_i-e_i)^2}{e_i} = 73.79726$

-   **Degree of freedom**: df = k - 1 - q = 3 - 1 - 0 = 2

-   **P-value:** P($\chi^2_2 \geq 73.79726$) \<\< 0.05

#### Decision

-   Since p \<\< 0.05, there is a strong evidence to reject the null hypothesis, people don't randomly choose the stall but rather having a specific preference towards the third stall.

#### Some external studies about behaviors 

In addition to our own investigation, external studies offer valuable perspectives that can deepen our understanding of student behavior. One such study by Heston (2023) delves into the psychology behind bathroom stall selection, revealing patterns that may have broader implications for how students make decisions in academic and lifestyle contexts.

Heston’s research found that people tend to choose the last stall, often due to the increased sense of privacy it provides. As Heston notes [@heston2023]:\
*"The findings in this study support the hypothesis that people seek security when choosing which bathroom stall to use. The top choice in this study, which occurred twice as often as the other two options, was the stall with a solid wall on one side and only one exposed partition. This preference makes psychological sense, as people are drawn to the stall that offers greater privacy than the others."*

This preference for privacy and security can be linked to broader student behaviors, particularly in academic and lifestyle choices. Just as students might opt for the bathroom stall that feels most secure, they may also seek out environments that provide comfort and control over their surroundings. In academic settings, this could manifest in students preferring smaller, quieter study spaces or choosing professors who create a more personalized, supportive classroom atmosphere. In lifestyle choices, it may reflect a tendency to gravitate toward habits or routines that offer a sense of stability and reduced uncertainty, whether it’s in their social interactions, study habits, or even daily schedules.

The strong evidence for systematic spatial choices has practical implications for university facility design. Students' clear preference for privacy-providing options suggests that learning spaces, study areas, and social environments should incorporate varied privacy levels to optimize student comfort and well-being.
